steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank joins two independent clauses: "The Great Salt Desert in western Asia is an arid [desert]" and "it is one of the largest deserts in the world, with a total area of about 77,000 km²." The phrase "for example" signals that the Great Salt Desert is an instance of an arid desert. The blank must properly separate the two independent clauses.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'desert, for example;': the comma after "desert" sets off "for example" as a parenthetical within the first clause, and the semicolon correctly separates the two independent clauses. ✓
  • Choice B — 'desert, for example,': two commas around "for example" leave nothing to separate the two independent clauses — a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — 'desert; for example,': the semicolon before "for example" is grammatically valid — it separates two independent clauses with "for example" acting as a conjunctive adverb. The punctuation itself is not the problem. The problem is logical: "for example" must introduce an instance of what precedes it. With C, the second clause ("it is one of the largest deserts in the world") would be presented as an example of the desert being arid — but sheer size is not an example of aridness. The logical relationship fails. ✗
  • Choice D — 'desert, for example': no punctuation after "for example" — the two independent clauses run together with no separation. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
